food_name: Ondeh Ondeh (Pandan Glutinous Rice Ball with Palm Sugar)
food_subgroup: Pandan glutinous rice ball with palm sugar filling, rolled in grated coconut

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Ondeh Ondeh. The nearest defensible parent is a glutinous rice confectionery or filled steamed rice ball. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this traditional Peranakan kueh.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based confectionery — glutinous rice ball with filling (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Ondeh Ondeh)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Ondeh Ondeh. A01XS used as closest defensible parent for glutinous rice-based confectionery with filling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Ondeh Ondeh or equivalent traditional Peranakan kueh.

serving.description: 4 pieces (approximately 100g); also commonly referenced as 1 serving of 60g (approximately 2–3 pieces)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ondeh Ondeh is 4 pieces (approximately 100g), providing 218 kcal, 41.5g carbohydrate, and 18.5g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Ondeh Ondeh — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F11090041
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F11090041 (Ondeh Ondeh). This is an exact match to the named food item. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 where HPB data was not available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glutinous Rice (Waxy Rice, Cooked) — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 78 derived from University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous (waxy) rice, cooked. Ondeh Ondeh is primarily composed of glutinous rice flour (the dominant starch component) and gula melaka (palm sugar). The GI of glutinous rice is well-established as high (~72–98 range in published literature). A value of 78 is used as a defensible central estimate. Glycemic load per 100g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (78 × 41.5) / 100 = 32.4. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: Ondeh Ondeh contains approximately 82mg sodium per 100g (4 pieces), representing about 4% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, making it one of the lowest-sodium traditional kueh in Singapore and generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension in moderate portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI 78) from glutinous rice flour combined with 18.5g sugar per 100g from gula melaka filling, producing a glycemic load of approximately 32 per 100g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit or avoid Ondeh Ondeh; the combination of high-GI glutinous rice flour (GI ~78) and a high-sugar palm sugar filling (18.5g sugar per 100g) produces a significant postprandial blood glucose spike, with a glycemic load of ~32 per 4-piece serving — well above the threshold for a high-GL food — and consumption should be restricted to 1–2 pieces as an occasional treat with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (4.5g per 100g) from grated coconut coating, and high refined carbohydrate and sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ondeh Ondeh contains 4.5g saturated fat per 100g primarily from the grated coconut coating, alongside 41.5g refined carbohydrate and 18.5g sugar, which may contribute to dyslipidaemia and elevated triglycerides with frequent consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional small portions and account for the saturated fat within their daily dietary fat budget.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content may promote systemic inflammation with frequent consumption
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Frequent consumption of Ondeh Ondeh may promote low-grade inflammation due to its high refined carbohydrate (41.5g) and sugar (18.5g) content; occasional consumption is acceptable, and the pandan component provides minor chlorophyll antioxidants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ondeh Ondeh (also spelled Onde Onde or Ondeh-Ondeh) is a beloved traditional Peranakan and Malay kueh (sweet confection) widely sold at kueh stalls, wet markets, and Peranakan restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia. Each piece is a small, bite-sized ball made from glutinous rice flour (tepung pulut) infused with fresh pandan (screwpine) juice, which gives the dough its characteristic vivid green colour and fragrant, grassy-sweet aroma. The ball is filled with a nugget of gula melaka (dark palm sugar), which dissolves into a liquid during boiling. The balls are boiled in water until they float — the traditional signal that the palm sugar inside has fully melted — then immediately rolled in freshly grated coconut that has been lightly salted. When bitten, the chewy, slightly sticky exterior gives way to a dramatic burst of warm, caramel-sweet palm sugar syrup. Nutritionally, Ondeh Ondeh provides 218 kcal per 100g (approximately 4 pieces), with 41.5g carbohydrate, 18.5g sugar, 5.2g fat (predominantly saturated fat 4.5g from coconut), 2.5g protein, and 1.8g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is high (~78) due to the waxy starch structure of glutinous rice flour, making it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium is low at 82mg per 100g. Ondeh Ondeh is naturally vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is a staple at Malay and Peranakan tea sessions, festive occasions including Hari Raya and Chinese New Year, and is considered one of the most visually striking and flavour-forward kueh in Singapore's traditional confectionery repertoire.

can a diabetic eat ondeh ondeh
ondeh ondeh vs ang ku kueh calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database under exact food code F11090041 (Ondeh Ondeh), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous rice (analogous estimate, confidence 0.9). Three minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 where HPB data was not available; all other values are at confidence 1.0 from the verified HPB source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Ondeh Ondeh not available in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (F11090041) or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source for this food code. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source F11090041). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero; Ondeh Ondeh contains no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero; this is a fully plant-based food with no animal products). vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero; no animal-derived or fortified ingredients present). copper_mg (0.08), manganese_mg (0.15), and vitamin_k_mcg (0.2) sourced from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 as these micronutrients were not individually reported in the HPB source. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous (waxy) rice, cooked; no direct GI measurement for Ondeh Ondeh as a composite food was found in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (78 × 41.5) / 100 = 32.4 per 100g (4-piece) serving. Two default portion sizes are recorded: 60g (1 serving, enriched_json source) and 100g (4 pieces, HPB verified source). The 100g / 4-piece portion is used as the primary serving for GL calculation. Hindi local name (ओंडे ओंडे) is a phonetic transliteration; no established Hindi culinary term exists for this Peranakan kueh.
